summary: Reis argues that organizations are failing to distinguish between Deep Work (design, architecture, thinking) and Delivery Work (shipping products), forcing both into rigid Sprint frameworks that don't accommodate the time and uncertainty inherent in creative work. This leads to accumulating technical debt patched over with 'bubble wrap and duct tape' rather than proper solutions, while practitioners feel unable to do quality work due to pressure to move fast.
key_points: Deep Work (design, architecture, modeling) requires intense focus and discovery that doesn't fit neatly into two-week Sprints
Most teams practice 'Scrum by name' or Scrumfall, ignoring iteration and review while technical debt backlogs grow indefinitely
The secular trend toward cheap, disposable output is accelerating with AI productivity expectations
Kanban or Sprint Spikes can shelter Deep Work from delivery pressure, but require leadership buy-in
Short-termism in leadership makes quality-focused change difficult when incentives reward speed over craftsmanship
money_quotes: I spoke with an engineering lead who described his job as 'adding more bubble wrap and duct tape' to his systems.
Instead of determining the best way to do certain types of work, we force work into something convenient, whether it's a good fit or not.

Whereas back in the day, people took pride in the quality of their deliverables, nowadays one's pride is associated with a clean burn down chart, working with an AI co-pilot that doesn't hallucinate too often, and not getting fired this week.
It's funny how people say they're operating Lean and Agile, and yet commit one of the cardinal sins of waste - rework and backlogs.
key_insight: The relentless pressure to deliver fast is destroying the conditions needed for quality work, turning engineering into an exercise in patching problems rather than solving them properly.
